title: Sheaves and contact non-squeezing in $\mathbb R^{2n} \times S^1$
speaker: Sheng-Fu Chiu
abstract: In this talk I will introduce a way to associate a triangulated category of sheaves with a domain of $\mathbb R^{2n} \times S^1$. The cohomological information on the category side helps to detect the contact non-squeezing property of the domain on the topology side.

proof_stages.label: Contact ball squeezing problem and threshold theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up R^{2n}×S^1 with contact form dz+p dq, defines contact balls B_R={p^2+q^2<R^2}×S^1, recalls the Eliashberg–Kim–Polterovich squeezing/non-squeezing picture, and states the intended strengthening that below size 1 balls can be squeezed while at size at least 1 they cannot.

proof_stages.label: Contravariant invariant and squeezing diagram
proof_stages.summary: Assuming a compactly supported contact isotopy squeezes a larger ball into a smaller one, the speaker proposes a contravariant contact invariant C(U) of domains, with induced maps on chain complexes, for which the inclusion map of the large ball factors through the squeezed image and the small ball.

proof_stages.label: Graded product obstruction
proof_stages.summary: In the cohomological endomorphism/Yoneda algebra one compares nonzero classes associated to the inclusion maps, and the product factorization forced by a squeeze would imply an impossible degree inequality once the auxiliary integer parameter is chosen large enough.

proof_stages.label: Microlocal sheaf model of contact domains
proof_stages.summary: The construction is moved to sheaves by lifting a contact domain to the universal cover R^{2n}×R and then conifying it inside T^*Y with positive zeta-coordinate, so that Kashiwara–Schapira singular support can encode the corresponding conic symplectic/Lagrangian data.

proof_stages.label: Semiorthogonal decomposition and projection kernels
proof_stages.summary: For the conified domain C U, the speaker defines sheaf subcategories microsupported away from or along C U, uses their orthogonality and distinguished-triangle decomposition to obtain projection functors represented by convolution kernels P_U and Q_U, and explains that contact isotopies act by sheaf quantization/conjugation on these kernels.

proof_stages.label: Finite cyclic equivariant invariant C_N(U)
proof_stages.summary: Because P_U initially sees the cover rather than the S^1 quotient, the construction is discretized with an N-fold cyclic action and the unit z-shift kernel T, defining C_N(U) as an equivariant RHom between N-fold convolution/tensor powers of P_U and the shift data, thereby producing a contact invariant of domains in R^{2n}×S^1.

proof_stages.label: Explicit computation for balls
proof_stages.summary: The relevant classes for balls are computed by unwrapping the sheaf operations, in a form analogous to generating-function calculations, identifying them with cohomology of finite-dimensional manifolds after the Z/N quotient and yielding both nonvanishing above the threshold and the ceiling-type degree formula needed for the obstruction.

proof_stages.label: Assembly of the non-squeezing proof
proof_stages.summary: Combining the convolutional sheaf invariant with the explicit nonvanishing and degree computation gives the promised contradiction to any squeeze, completing the sheaf-theoretic proof of contact non-squeezing above the size-one threshold.

examples.name: Radii tending down to the threshold
examples.note: An audience member asks about taking radii such as R^2=1+1/n, prompting the speaker to clarify that the degree is a jumping integer depending on the auxiliary cyclic parameter N, not a strictly increasing real-valued function of the radius.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker takes the proposed radii sequence near 1 and analyzes how the degree function behaves, invoking the ceiling formula and jump behavior to answer the objection

passes.reason: a sequence of radii approaching the critical threshold is a limiting boundary case used to challenge the degree argument.
the sequence of radii approaching the critical value 1 is a limiting boundary case used to challenge the naive degree-growth argument
A sequence of radii approaching the critical threshold is a boundary case used to challenge the naive degree-growth argument.
The radii approach the critical radius-one threshold and are posed as an enemy case for the degree-jump argument.
The radii approach the critical non-squeezing threshold and are raised as an enemy case for the naive degree-monotonicity argument.
the radii approach the critical squeezing threshold and are raised as an enemy case for a fixed integer-valued degree argument
A sequence of radii approaching the critical threshold is used as an adversarial edge case for the degree-jump argument.
Used as an adversarial sequence of radii approaching the critical threshold to challenge how integer-valued degrees can keep distinguishing them.
the sequence of radii approaching the critical value is used as an adversarial test of the integer-degree argument and is structurally a limiting threshold case
The sequence of radii approaching the critical threshold is used as an edge/adversarial case for the integer-degree argument.
a sequence of radii approaching the critical threshold is used as an adversarial near-boundary test for the degree argument.
a decreasing sequence of radii approaching the critical threshold is used as an adversarial test of the claimed degree-jump argument and is structurally a limiting boundary case.

examples.name: Representation-theoretic convolution algebras
examples.note: The speaker compares the desired sheaf-convolution product with familiar convolution descriptions of group algebras such as Weyl-group algebras and related representation-theoretic algebras, serving only as an analogy for the Yoneda/convolution product.
examples.engagement_reason: representation-theoretic convolution algebras are cited as an analogy/source of motivation, with examples like Weyl group algebras named but not computed or applied

passes.reason: standard convolution algebras from representation-theoretic correspondences are used as a motivating analogy.
standard representation-theoretic convolution/Ext algebras are invoked as a motivating analogy for the sheaf convolution product
Representation-theoretic convolution algebras are standard members of a systematic theory and are used only as an analogy.
Representation-theoretic convolution algebras are used as a standard motivating analogy rather than to refute a claim.
These are standard convolution algebras from correspondences in representation theory, used only as an analogy for the sheaf convolution/Yoneda product.
these are standard systematic convolution-algebra examples from representation theory used only as an analogy
Convolution algebras from representation theory are invoked as a standard motivating analogy, not as a sporadic or extremal object.
Convolution algebras from representation theory are invoked as a standard analogy for the sheaf-convolution algebra structure, not as an obstruction.
representation-theoretic convolution algebras are invoked as a standard analogy for the sheaf convolution/Ext-algebra story
Representation-theoretic convolution algebras are cited as a standard motivating analogy, not as an obstruction or sporadic object.
representation-theoretic convolution algebras are invoked only as a motivating analogy and are structurally ordinary for their setting.
representation-theoretic convolution algebras are invoked as a standard analogy for the sheaf convolution construction, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Orthogonal subspaces in a Hilbert-space analogy
examples.note: The semiorthogonal sheaf subcategories are explained by analogy with orthogonal subspaces of a vector space or L^2 space, where RHom plays the role of an inner product and projection functors mimic orthogonal projections.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker develops the Hilbert-space orthogonal-subspace analogy by translating inner products to RHom, orthogonality to vanishing RHom, decompositions to distinguished triangles, and projections to convolution kernels

passes.reason: ordinary orthogonal subspaces are used only as a Hilbert-space analogy for RHom-orthogonality.
ordinary orthogonal subspaces serve as a Hilbert-space analogy for RHom as an inner product and for categorical decomposition
Ordinary orthogonal subspaces serve as a Hilbert-space analogy for categorical orthogonality, not as a refutation.
Orthogonal Hilbert subspaces serve as an ordinary explanatory analogy for RHom-orthogonality of categories.
Orthogonal subspaces are an ordinary Hilbert-space model for semiorthogonal sheaf subcategories, not a sporadic, limiting, extremal, or maximally symmetric object.
ordinary orthogonal subspaces are used as a Hilbert-space analogy for RHom-orthogonality
Orthogonal Hilbert-space subspaces are used as an explanatory analogy for RHom-orthogonality, with no counterexample role or special classification status.
Orthogonal subspaces are used as an ordinary Hilbert-space analogy for semiorthogonal subcategories and RHom-orthogonality.
orthogonal subspaces serve as an ordinary Hilbert-space analogy for perpendicular subcategories and RHom
Orthogonal subspaces illustrate the zero-overlap/maximal-separation case in the Hilbert-space analogy.
orthogonal subspaces serve as an analogy, with orthogonality giving zero or minimal overlap among pairs of subspaces.
orthogonal Hilbert-space subspaces serve as an ordinary analogy for semiorthogonal categorical decompositions.

examples.name: Testing displaceability by RHom
examples.note: As an optional application of the same sheaf method, the speaker mentions assigning sheaves to two subsets and using RHom, with attention to conjugation under Hamiltonian/contact motion, to detect non-displaceability.
examples.engagement_reason: testing displaceability by RHom is only sketched as a possible application for two subsets, without carrying out the test on a concrete pair or computing the RHom

passes.reason: RHom between sheaves associated to subsets is proposed as an obstruction detecting non-displaceability.
RHom between sheaf objects associated to subsets is proposed as an obstruction detecting failure of displacement
RHom between sheaves associated to subsets is proposed as an obstruction for proving non-displaceability.
The RHom comparison is a standard sheaf-theoretic invariant invoked as an obstruction to displacing subsets.
The RHom complex is a standard derived-Hom object, proposed as an obstruction detecting non-displaceability of subsets.
nonzero RHom is proposed as an obstruction to a Hamiltonian displacement, hence to show displaceability fails
Using RHom to compare sheaves of subsets is presented as a generic application to displaceability questions rather than a concrete obstruction example.
RHom between sheaf objects is proposed as an invariant to obstruct displacement, hence to prove non-displaceability.
RHom is proposed as an obstruction detecting non-displaceability of subsets, while the construction itself is structurally standard
RHom is proposed as an invariant that can obstruct displacement and thus detect non-displaceability.
RHom between sheaf quantizations is proposed as an obstruction to displacing subsets, with no special subset structure specified.
using RHom to test displaceability is presented as a generic application of the invariant rather than a specific obstructing example.
